reasoning:
The 10-year Treasury yield (WGS10YR) closed at 4.02% for the week ending February 27, 2026. This is at the very bottom of its 1-year trailing range, which has consistently seen support at the 4.00% to 4.02% levels (e.g., late October and late November 2025 where subsequent weeks rebounded above 4.10%). 

The FRED linear trend analysis projects a reversion to approximately 4.12% and assigns a ~70% probability of being above 4.02% by early March. While short-term downward momentum could persist (it fell from 4.26% in early February), historical mean-reversion suggests a bounce is more likely than a continued breakdown, given that fundamentals and inflation expectations have not radically collapsed. 

For the short-term resolution (March 8, using the March 6 data), the localized support level at 4.00% strongly favors an upward bounce or stabilization above 4.02% (Probability: 0.64). For the longer-term resolution (March 31, using March 27 data), mean reversion logic still holds, but increasing uncertainty over a longer time horizon dictates a return closer to 50/50, bringing the estimate down to 0.58.
